sections.heading: f(n) = g(n) + h(n)
sections.body: A* combines the strengths of UCS and Greedy Best First Search into one evaluation function: f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where g(n) is the cost already spent reaching n, and h(n) is the estimated cost remaining to the goal. It always expands the node with the lowest f(n).

sections.heading: Cost Function
sections.body: By combining both the cost so far and the estimated cost remaining, A* balances "don't waste what I've already spent getting here" (UCS's concern) against "but also head toward the goal efficiently" (Greedy's concern) — getting the benefits of both.

sections.heading: Optimality
sections.body: A* is optimal (guaranteed to find the cheapest solution) when its heuristic is admissible — never overestimating the true remaining cost — because that guarantees A* never prematurely commits to a path that only looks cheap due to an overly optimistic heuristic.

sections.heading: Completeness
sections.body: A* is complete on finite state spaces with positive step costs, and generally complete on infinite spaces too as long as there's a solution and costs don't shrink to zero along any infinite path — guaranteeing it won't loop forever without ever finding an existing solution.

sections.heading: Complexity
sections.body: A*'s time and space complexity are still exponential in the worst case, but a well-designed heuristic can dramatically reduce this in practice compared to uninformed search — the quality of the heuristic directly determines how much better than brute-force search A* performs.

sections.heading: Applications
sections.body: A* is one of the most widely used search algorithms in practice — powering pathfinding in video games and robotics navigation, GPS route planning, and puzzle-solving, precisely because it's both optimal (with a good heuristic) and typically much faster than uninformed alternatives.

quiz.question: A* is guaranteed optimal when:
options: It uses no heuristic at all
It uses a stack instead of a priority queue

quiz.question: Why is A* widely used in practice (e.g. pathfinding, GPS)?
options: It combines optimality (with a good heuristic) with typically much better efficiency than uninformed search
It never needs a goal test
